segments.s132-g73: همه مهتران (subject) + کهتر او شدند (predicate): the phrase reads 'all the chiefs became lesser than him.' Classical Persian here omits the expected preposition از ('than'), so کهترِ او شد(ند) means 'became inferior to him.' شدند is the past plural copula 'became/were.' Word order is subject → predicate.
segments.s132-g74: پرستنده و چاکر او شدند is a coordinated predicate continuing the idea of subordination: 'they became his worshippers and his retainers.' پرستنده ('worshipper/devoted follower') is a deverbal noun used as an agent noun; چاکر is a noun 'servant/retainer.' او (to him) marks whose worshippers/retainers they became, and شدند is repeated as the past plural copula applying to both coordinated nouns.
segments.s132-g75: کجا introduces a rhetorical/conditional clause ('where/if'), followed by پادشا دادگر بود 'there was a just king' (پادشا = king, دادگر = just). بود is the past copula 'was.' و بس ('and enough' or 'and that suffices') is an elliptical comment meaning 'that alone suffices.' The construction is classical and terse.
segments.s132-g76: نیازش ('his need' with possessive suffix) + نیاید (negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not arise/does not come') + بفریادرس ('to a rescuer' or 'to call a rescuer'): together they mean 'he has no need of a rescuer' or literally 'his need does not come to a rescuer.' Classical Persian uses نیاید in this sense to say a need does not arise.
segments.s132-g77: بدین داستان گفتم combines ب- (here 'به'/'in' prefixed) + این to form 'بدین' = 'with this / in this' and داستان 'tale' with گفتم 'I said / I told' (classical past). آن کم شنود is a compact relative-like phrase: آن = 'the one' / 'those', کم = 'little/few', شنود (classical form of hears) functions as 'have heard' or 'hear scantily' — i.e. 'those who have heard little of it.' The clause is elliptical, preparing the next line.
segments.s132-g78: کنون 'now' + رزم رستم is a genitive (the battle of Rostam) with the possessor after the possessed (typical noun-noun construct). بباید is the modal 'must/ought' (classical form with b- prefix), and سرود here is a verbal-noun / object 'song/ode' meaning 'must be (now) celebrated/told.' The whole line: 'Now the battle of Rostam must be sung/related.'
segments.s132-g41: فروماند is a past/intransitive predicate placed before the subject (a common poetic order) meaning 'was cast down/worn out.' کاووس is the subject (proper name). The clause continues with a coordinated small clause تشویر خورد: here خورد is the past of خوردن used in a light-verb construction meaning 'befell/was suffered,' so تشویر (a noun meaning a shameful condition) + خورد = 'suffered shame.'
segments.s132-g42: ازان is a referential preposition/pronoun 'of those/from those' linking this phrase to what caused the shame. نامداران is the plural noun 'renowned ones/celebrated men.' روز نبرد is a genitive/construct: 'the day of battle' (روز 'day' modified by نبرد 'battle'), giving the temporal/occasional frame.
segments.s132-g43: بسیچید is a past verb meaning 'gathered/mustered' (no explicit subject expressed—pro-drop is normal). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' اندر is the preposition 'in/within,' عماری denotes a building or pavilion, and نشست is the past of نشستن 'sat/settled,' here describing his taking a seat within that structure.
segments.s132-g44: پشیمانی و درد is a coordinated subject 'remorse and pain.' بودش is بود with the enclitic ش ('it was to him' → 'he had / he was held by'); the construction means these emotions were upon him. بدست is literal 'in (his) hand' but idiomatically here indicates being seized or possessed by those feelings.
segments.s132-g45: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came' (چو = 'when'). بر تخت 'onto the throne' (بر preposition + تخت 'throne'). و links گاه بلند, here meaning 'the lofty seat/occasion'—the phrase presents the exalted place/time of his return; word order (verb before subject/place) reflects poetic flexibility.
segments.s132-g46: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش). بود is the past copula 'was.' زان is a causal/relational preposition 'from/ because of that.' کار is 'affair/deed.' مانده is a participial/past adjective 'remaining/left,' and نژند is an adjectival predicate 'base/worthless' — together 'his heart was, because of that deed, left base.'
segments.s132-g47: چهل روز is a temporal phrase 'forty days.' بر پیش یزدان is an adverbial phrase 'in the presence of/before God' (بر + پیش + یزدان). به پای indicates manner 'on foot' or 'by foot' (showing how he spent those days).
segments.s132-g48: بپیمود is a past tense verb 'he traversed/covered' (ب- prefix + پیمود). خاک is the object 'the earth/dust' (i.e., travelled over the land). و coordinates the next verb بپرداخت, which here means 'rendered/paid (homage or attention),' and جای 'place' is its object—together indicating he paid respects at places or attended to sacred sites.
segments.s132-g65: جهان (subject) + گفتی here functions as a verbal noun/quote marker ('it is said' / 'they say')—a poetic usage introducing what follows. از داد دیبا شدست is a free word order expression: از داد = 'from/by that justice', دیبا = 'brocade' (metaphorical), شدست is an archaic past form meaning 'became' (Classical Persian past 3sg). The clause literally says 'the world, it is said, from that justice became brocade.'
segments.s132-g66: همان شاه = 'that same king' (demonstrative + noun). بر گاه is a prepositional phrase 'on the seat/at the throne' (بر = 'upon', گاه used here as a locative/occasional 'place'—poetic for court/seat). زیبا = adjective 'beautiful/splendid' modifies شاه implicitly, and شدست is again the archaic past 'became' (3rd person). Word order is emphatic: demonstrative + noun, locative phrase, predicate.
segments.s132-g67: ز = poetic form of از ('from'). هر کشوری = 'every country/realm' (هر quantifier + adjectival/genitive form کشوری). نامور مهتری is a noun phrase where نامور ('renowned') modifies مهتری ('chief/elder, i.e., leading man'). The whole phrase is an NP roughly 'a renowned chief from every country.'
segments.s132-g68: که = relative pronoun 'who/which'. بر سر = 'on the head' (prepositional phrase). نهادی here functions as a past action 'had placed' (classical past of نهادن) — in poetry the form appears as نهادی; بلند modifies افسری ('a high/tall diadem/crown'). The clause literally: 'who had set a lofty diadem upon (their) heads.'
segments.s132-g69: به درگاه = 'to the court/threshold' (به + درگاه); کاووس شاه is the name with title in apposition ('Kavus, the king'). آمدند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they came'. Word order is prepositional goal + person + verb.
segments.s132-g70: وزان = 'and from that' (poetic combination of و + از + آن). سرکشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'to lift one's head' and idiomatically 'to rebel' (here: initiate revolt). به راه آمدند = 'they took to the road / set out' (به راه = on the road; آمدند = they came), so the sequence says that, from that moment, they rose in rebellion and went on the road. The verbal noun + آمدند combination expresses the manner/result of their coming.
segments.s132-g71: زمانه چنان شد = 'the times became such' (زمانه subject, چنان شد past 'became so'). که بود از نخست is a comparative/relative clause: که = 'that/which', بود = past 'was', از نخست = 'from the first/at the beginning' — together 'as it had been from the first.' This is a standard classical structure where شد + که + بود expresses 'became such that it was...'
segments.s132-g72: به آب وفا = 'with/to the water of fidelity' (به marking instrument/means here). رویِ خسرو = 'the face of Khosrow' (possessive construction; روی = face). بشست is classical past 3rd singular 'he washed' (note the simple classical past form rather than modern compound). Word order places the instrument/means before the object and then the verb: 'he washed Khosrow's face with the water of fidelity.'
segments.s132-g33: همان کن is an imperative phrase meaning “do the same / do likewise” (همان modifies the imperative کن). که introduces a relative clause (“that/which”) whose verb comes at the end: بیدار شاهان کنند = “vigilant/awake kings do.” Note the plural present subjunctive/indicative کنَند at the clause end (poetic word order).
segments.s132-g34: This is the remainder of the relative clause: two plural noun subjects (ستاینده و نیک‌خواهان) linked by و, with the plural verb کنند understood as their predicate. In other words: “(as) the praisers and the well‑wishers do.”
segments.s132-g35: جز از ... مجوی is an exception construction: “seek nothing except …”. بندگی پیش یزدان = “devotion/servitude in the presence of God.” مجوی is a negative imperative/command (‘do not seek’ / ‘you should not seek’), so the clause reads “do not seek anything except devotion before God.”
segments.s132-g36: مزن is a negative imperative “do not (put/strike)”; دست در نیک و بد is an idiomatic phrase meaning “put your hand into good and bad” = “interfere in judging/handling good and evil.” جز بدوی qualifies the manner: “except according to your بدوی (original/innate) sense/judgment.”
segments.s132-g37: چنین داد پاسخ = “thus gave an answer” (داد = gave, followed by the noun پاسخ). که begins the reported content: از راستی indicates source/measure (“from truth / by truth”); the clause continues in the next segment.
segments.s132-g38: نیاید is a negative present/optative meaning “should not / must not come” (poetic form). به کار اندرون = “into the inner affairs/household matters.” کاستی = “deficiency / lack”; thus: “no lack should come into the inner affairs by/from truth.”
segments.s132-g39: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle meaning “continually / kept on.” داد here is the past verb “gave” used with گفتی (speech/words) to mean “gave speech / spoke” (i.e. kept speaking). The coordinate clause و بیداد نیست states “and injustice is not (present).”
segments.s132-g40: ز نام تو is a prepositional phrase “from/by your name (reputation).” جان من آزاد نیست is the main clause: “my life/soul is not free.” The construction means figuratively that the speaker’s life is not free of (or is bound by) the addressee’s name/reputation.
segments.s132-g57: پشیمان شد is a two-word predicate: پشیمان (adjective) + شد (past of شدن) = “became regretful/repentant.” و simply coordinates clauses. درد بگزید is a poetic causative/metaphorical phrasing where بگزید (literally “bit/stung”) takes درد as its subject/experiencer: “pain bit/seized him.” رنج is a nominal complement added by coordination (and suffering).
segments.s132-g58: نهاده is a perfective/past participial form of نهادن meaning “having put/placed” (here: ‘having set aside’); it functions adverbially/parenthetically. ببخشید is past tense meaning “gave/bestowed” (here: to give away). بسیار modifies گنج and means “many/much.” گنج is the direct object “treasure(s).” Word order is participial clause + main verb + object.
segments.s132-g59: همی is a continuous/iterative adverb (‘continually/kept on’). رخ (face) is the direct object of بمالید, the past-tense/formal-poetic verb meaning ‘rubbed’ or ‘pressed (his face).’ بر تیره خاک is a prepositional phrase ‘upon the dark earth/dust’ (بر + adjective + noun). The verb is narrative past, describing a repeated physical action.
segments.s132-g60: نیایش کنان is a verbal-adverbial participial construction: نیایش (prayer) + کنان (the participle ‘doing’) = ‘praying.’ پیش is the preposition ‘before/in the presence of.’ یزدان پاک is the noun phrase ‘the pure God’; پاک is an adjective postposed to yizdān. Together this gives the manner and addressee of the action: he rubbed his face while praying before the pure God.
segments.s132-g61: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when/after.’ بگذشت is the past tense ‘passed’ (time passed). یک چند is an adverbial phrase ‘a little while’ or ‘somewhat/for a short while.’ گریان is a participial/nominal form ‘weeping,’ and چنین (‘thus/so’) describes the manner — together: ‘when a short while passed, weeping thus.’
segments.s132-g62: ببخشود is a past-tense verb meaning ‘forgave’ (here the action of pardoning). بر وی is a prepositional phrase ‘toward/to him’ (بر + وی). جهان‌آفرین is the agent/nominative ‘the Creator/the world‑maker’ and is the subject performing the forgiveness. Word order is verb + indirect object + subject (poetic inversion).
segments.s132-g63: یکی functions as an indefinite subject (‘someone’/‘a certain one’). داد here is a substantive meaning ‘justice’ or ‘a just order.’ نو is the adjective ‘new’ modifying داد. ساخت is the past-tense verb ‘made/established.’ اندر جهان is the locative prepositional phrase ‘in the world.’ The clause thus says that a new justice was set up in the world.
segments.s132-g64: که is a relative/conjunctive particle ‘which/that’ linking to the preceding noun (the new justice). تابنده is an adjective/participial form ‘shining/resplendent.’ شد is the past of شدن ‘became.’ بر introduces the scope/location ‘over/on.’ کهان و مهان form a paired noun phrase indicating wide extent or important people/realms; together the phrase means the justice became radiant/manifest across the domains and among the great. Word order is relative clause following the noun it qualifies.
segments.s132-g49: همی is a continuous/progressive marker ('kept, was still'); ریخت is the past verb 'poured/flowed' used here intransitively; از introduces the source ('from'); دیدگان is the plural noun 'eyes' (source of the flow); آب زرد is a noun phrase 'yellow/pale water' (a poetic word for tears) with the adjective زرد after the noun, a normal Persian adjective order in older/poetic style.
segments.s132-g50: همی again marks repeated/continuous action; از here introduces the object of یاد کردن (the person recalled) in the pattern «از X یاد کردن» = 'to remember X'; جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑creator/Creator'; یاد is the noun 'remembrance' and together with کرد (the past tense of کردن) forms 'he remembered' (literally 'he made remembrance of the Creator').
segments.s132-g51: ز شرم is a causal phrase 'out of/because of shame'; از در کاخ is a locative source 'from the door/gate of the palace' (از + در + محل); بیرون نرفت is the negative past 'he did not go out' with the verb نرفت placed at the end, as Persian places the verb after its complements in this poetic register.
segments.s132-g52: همی marks repeated/ongoing action ('kept'); پوست ('skin') functions as the (figurative) subject/topic here; گفتی is an archaic/poetic form related to 'saying' (in context it functions as 'said' or 'saying' introducing direct speech); برو is the quoted imperative 'go'; بر + به + کفت yields the locative phrase 'onto/into the palm' (بر = onto, به = to/toward, کفت = 'palm' here). The whole clause is a quoted or reported exclamation the skin (figuratively) utters.
segments.s132-g53: همی marks continuous action; ریخت is the past 'poured/flowed'; از introduces the source 'from'; دیده پالوده is a noun + past-participial adjective 'eye(s) purified/cleansed' (adjective follows the noun); خون is the theme 'blood' that is said to pour from the eyes — poetic inversion places source and modifier before the noun 'خون'.
segments.s132-g54: همی again signals repeated action; خواست is the past tense 'he sought/wanted'; آمرزش is the direct object 'forgiveness' and رهنمون (here in apposition) is 'guidance/lead' — together the phrase means 'he sought forgiveness and guidance.' Word order is verb after the continuous marker and its objects follow the verb.
segments.s132-g55: ز شرم 'because of shame' gives the cause; دلیران منش is a genitive/compound 'the demeanor/character of the brave' (دلیران modifies منش); کرد is the past tense 'made/did' used causatively here; پست is the predicate 'low/base' — so the clause means he caused the brave men's character/behaviour to become low/humbled.
segments.s132-g56: خرام is a verb/noun meaning 'he strode/pranced' (describing a proud gait); و is the coordinating 'and'; در بار here is the compound sense pointing to the court/royal presence (در used as 'door/gate' or locative element plus بار forming 'darbar' = court); دادن is the infinitive 'to give' and ببست is the past 'he closed/shut' — together the clause says he strode proudly and closed off giving (at the court) or 'shut the court's giving' (i.e. stopped distribution).
segments.s132-g9: چو functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. کاووس is the proper name 'Kāvus'. نشنیدم is the past first-person of 'شنیدن' used here as 'I heard of/learned about' (classical use: to hear of someone). اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' (اندر = in/within, جهان = world) modifying the preceding clause.
segments.s132-g10: ندیدم is the first-person past 'I did not see' (here taken idiomatically as 'I saw no one'). کس is 'anyone/someone' used under negation ('no one'). از marks the subset/among ('from/among'). کهتران and مهان are nouns 'the low-born/lesser' and 'the great/nobles' respectively; they form a coordinated noun phrase 'from the low-born and the great' that کس scopes over.
segments.s132-g11: خرد نیست او را is an experiential/existential negation literally 'wisdom is not to him' (i.e. 'he has no wisdom'); note the inversion where خرد (the predicate) precedes نیست and او را marks the afflicted person ('to him'). نه ... نه introduces a correlative negative pair 'neither ... nor ...' listing دانش (knowledge) and رای (opinion/counsel).
segments.s132-g12: Another correlative negative: نه هوشش بجایست و نه دل بجای = 'neither his intellect is in its place nor his heart is in its place.' هوشش is 'his intellect' (possessive -ش), بجایست is a predicative adjective/phrase 'is fitting/appropriate' or 'is in order', و joins the two negative members, and بجای here means 'in place' (in the sense 'properly located/ordered').
segments.s132-g13: رسیدند is the past plural 'they arrived' (verb before subject, a common Persian word order). پس is an adverb 'then/after'. پهلوانان is the plural subject 'the champions/heroes', modified by the adjective بدوی which follows the noun in typical Persian order and here carries the sense 'original/ancient'.
segments.s132-g14: This segment lists adjectival qualities of those champions: نکوهش‌گر 'censuring/reproving', و 'and' joins items, تیز 'sharp/keen', و again 'and', پرخاشجوی 'aggressive/quarrelsome'. These adjectives follow the noun they modify and are coordinated by و.
segments.s132-g15: بدو is the clitic/short form meaning 'to him' (indirect object 'to him'), placed before the verb. گفت is the past tense 'said' (verb), with گودرز as the subject following the verb (V S O order). بیمارستان is the direct object of 'said' — the thing being spoken ('a hospital').
segments.s132-g16: ترا is the archaic/poetic object/benefactive 'for you' (here split into ت + را in the word list); جای is 'place', and زیباتر is the comparative 'more beautiful' (formed with the -تر comparative). از introduces the standard of comparison 'than/from', and شارستان is 'town/city' — together 'a place for you more beautiful than a town.'
segments.s132-g17: به (preposition) + دشمن (object) marks the beneficiary/recipient 'to the enemy'. دهی is a classical/poetic 2nd-person present/present-subjunctive form 'you give' (here meaning 'you give up' or 'you hand over'). هر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at every time / always'. جای خویش is the direct object 'one's own place' formed from جای 'place' + خویش (possessive formed from خوی + enclitic ش); the enclitic ش marks possession ('your/his/its'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (prepositional phrase before the verb, then temporal phrase, then the possessed noun).
segments.s132-g18: نگویی here is the negative form (ن + گویی) functioning as a prohibitive/negative present: 'do not say / do not tell'. به کس is the prepositional object 'to anyone'; بیهده modifies رای as an adjective 'idle/futile/folly'. رای خویش = 'your counsel/opinion' with خویش (خوی + enclitic ش) marking possession. The phrase is an admonition: 'do not tell anyone your foolish counsel.'
segments.s132-g19: سه بارت is an adverbial expression 'three times' (بار + enclitic ت in classical usage gives a sense of 'upon you / times experienced'); چنین qualifies the following nouns 'such' or 'so'. رنج و سختی is a coordinated noun phrase 'suffering and hardship'. فتاد is past tense 'befell/occurred' (literally 'fell' with the sense 'happened to').
segments.s132-g20: سرت is a locative/advantage phrase 'on/over you' (literally 'your head' used idiomatically 'to you'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. آزمایش = 'trial/ordeal'. نگشت is the negative past of گشت (ن + گشت) meaning 'did not turn / did not become' (here 'did not become [a master]'). اوستاد is the predicate noun 'master/teacher' (so the clause reads roughly 'from the trial you did not become a master').
segments.s132-g21: کشیدی is the past 2nd-person singular 'you drew/you led' (verb placed earlier here for poetic inversion). سپه را: سپه 'army' with the object marker را marking it as definite/direct object. به مازندران indicates the destination 'to Mazandaran'. The usual prose order would be سپه را به مازندران کشیدی, but poetic inversion places the verb first.
segments.s132-g22: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'see!'. تا introduces the subordinate clause here with the sense 'so that / to' (i.e. 'see how...'), and چه is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what/how'. سختی رسید اندران = 'hardship reached them / befell them' where رسید is past 'reached' and اندران is a postpositional phrase 'among them' (اندر + ان).
segments.s132-g23: دگرباره = 'again/anew'. مهمان دشمن شدی is a predicate phrase 'you became the enemy's guest' (مهمان 'guest', دشمن 'enemy', شدی past 'you became'). The sentence states that, once more, the addressee has fallen into the enemy's hospitality (i.e. been taken captive or hosted by the foe).
segments.s132-g24: صنم بودی: صنم 'idol' with بودی past 'you were' (meaning 'you were an idolater' or 'you were like an idol'); اکنون 'now' introduces the contrast. برهمن شدی 'you became a Brahmin' (برهمن 'Brahmin', شدی past 'you became'). The clause contrasts a former state with a new one ('you used to be an idol (or idolater); now you have become a Brahmin').
segments.s132-g25: به گیتی = a locative phrase: به + گیتی = 'in the world'. جز ... نماند is an exception construction: جز از پاک یزدان نماند literally 'there did not remain (anyone) except the Pure God.' نماند is the past-tense verb ماندن with the negative prefix ن- used impersonally ('there remained no one').
segments.s132-g26: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause referring back to 'no one': 'who ...'. منشور تیغ is a compound genitive ('the proclamation/charter of the sword') where the possessive -ت (your) and the object-marking را follow. ترا here is split orthographically into ت (2nd-person possessive suffix attached to تیغ: 'thy sword') + را (definite object marker). برنخواند is the past negative of خواندن with the prefix بر- here meaning 'to call/summon' or 'to read aloud', so the clause means 'who would not summon/read (you) by the proclamation.'
segments.s132-g27: به جنگ = 'into/for war' (prepositional phrase introducing the action). زمین gives the theatre 'the earth/land' (locative sense). سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'head to head' or 'all over, in direct clash.' تاختی is the simple past, 2nd-person singular of تاختن ('you charged/galloped'), so the whole segment = 'you charged into war across the land, head to head.'
segments.s132-g28: کنون = 'now'. باسمان here is most likely a proper name (a person, e.g. 'Basman') rather than the common noun. نیز = 'also'. پرداختی is the past 2nd-person form of پرداختن; in this epic sense it means 'you set upon/attacked/engaged' (here rendered 'you came to engage Basman'). Grammatically it continues the narration of your actions in the past.
segments.s132-g29: پس از تو = 'after you' (پس meaning 'after' here + از marking the relation). بدین is a fused prepositional/adverbial form meaning 'in this way' ('بـ' + 'دین' in the orthography); it functions adverbially 'thus/thisly'. داستانی is the noun 'a tale/story'. کنند is the present-subjunctive/formal present used here to mean 'they make/say' (they will tell). The clause means 'After you they will tell such a story.'
segments.s132-g30: که introduces the content of the tale ('that ...'). شاهی is an abstract/noun meaning 'kingship' or 'a kingly rule' and is the subject. برآمد is past tense 'arose/appeared'. به چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'to the high/lofty wheel/sky' (چرخ = heaven/sky, a poetic image for high station). Together: 'that kingship arose to the lofty heavens.'
segments.s132-g31: که again introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). تا here functions as 'so that/up to' (marking the extent or goal): 'so that he may gaze as far as the moon and sun.' ماه و خورشید را uses را as the definite object marker. بنگرد is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person of نگریستن 'to look/gaze' (here 'may look' or 'be able to see').
segments.s132-g32: ستاره is 'star' (subject or direct object of بشمرد depending on emphasis). یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one / each and every.' همی is an archaic particle often used in epic Persian to mark habitual/continuous or emphatic aspect (here giving the sense 'would/does indeed'). بشمرد is the present/subjunctive/optative 3rd-person of شمردن 'to count' — 'he counts/would count' or 'he will count.' The clause: 'he counts the stars one by one.'
segments.s132-g1: همی + کرد is a habitual/simple past construction meaning 'kept doing' or 'was doing continually' (همی marks continuous or iterative aspect). پوزش is the direct object 'pardon/excuse'; ز بهر گناه is a prepositional phrase 'because of / for the sin' (ز here marks reason/connection, بهر = 'for').
segments.s132-g2: This clause follows the object–verb pattern typical of classical Persian: مر = 'me / on my behalf' (dative), او را = 'him' (را marks a definite object), همی جست = 'continually searched/sought' (همی habitual, جست = 'sought'), هر سو سپاه = 'armies on every side' (هر سو = 'every side', سپاه = subject placed after the verb).
segments.s132-g3: خبر یافت زو: یافت is the past form 'found/learned' and takes خبر as its object ('learned the news'). زو = 'of/from him' (classical z + pronoun), so the whole phrase means 'they heard/learned news about him.' The proper names (رستم و گیو و طوس) are the logical agents (who learned the news).
segments.s132-g4: برفتند is the simple past plural 'they went/set out.' با + لشکری = 'with an army/host' (با = 'with'), گشن is an adjective 'hungry' qualifying لشکری, and کوس (connected by و) names the war-drums/signals that accompanied them. Word order is verb → accompanying phrase (با ...) typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s132-g5: به رستم = 'to Rostam' (به marks the indirect object/dative). چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus, so'); گفت is the past of 'to say' (the provided sense list for گفت in the data does not show the verbal sense, so the appropriate verbal sense is not listed). گودرز پیر is the speaker ('Goudarz the elder').
segments.s132-g6: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که (that/since). تا functions as 'until / from the time that' here and is followed by کرد (past 'made/caused') — together indicating a past time limit: 'from the time that my mother made ...'. مادرم را is the object 'my mother' (the possessive suffix م is attached to مادر and then the object marker را follows; the data split that suffix separately). سیر شیر = 'satiated with milk' (i.e. nursed to fullness); شیر = 'milk.'
segments.s132-g7: همی بینم is present habitual/continuous 'I see / I still see' (همی marking ongoing aspect, بینم first-person present). اندر جهان = 'in the world' (اندر = in, جهان = world). تاج و تخت = 'crown and throne' (a common pair meaning kingship/sovereignty).
segments.s132-g8: کیان و بزرگان = 'the Kian kings and the great ones.' بیدار بخت is a compound-like phrase: بیدار 'awake/alert' modifies بخت 'fortune/fate,' so together it carries the sense 'whose fortune is awake' or simply 'fortunate/awakened in destiny.' In classical Persian such adjective–noun juxtapositions describe a lasting condition (here: fortunate leaders).

sentences.s132-s37: All the chiefs became inferior to him; they became his worshippers and retainers.
sentences.s132-s38: Where there was a just king — that alone was enough; his need would not come to a rescuer.
sentences.s132-s39: I have told this tale; for those who have heard little, now the battle of Rostam must be sung.
sentences.s132-s21: Kavus was cast down and suffered disgrace at the hands of those famed warriors on the day of battle.
sentences.s132-s22: He mustered a great host and sat down inside a pavilion; remorse and sorrow seized him.
sentences.s132-s23: When he came to the throne and the lofty seat, his heart, because of that affair, remained base.
sentences.s132-s24: For forty days, before God and on foot, he traversed the earth and paid homage to the places.
sentences.s132-s33: It is said the world was turned into brocade by that justice; that same king became splendid upon the throne.
sentences.s132-s34: From every land came a famed chief,
sentences.s132-s35: they came to the court of King Kavus, and from that time, lifting their heads in revolt, they took to the road.
sentences.s132-s36: Time became such as it had been at the first; Khosrow washed his face in the water of fidelity.
sentences.s132-s17: Do the same that vigilant kings do, as do the praisers and the well‑wishers.
sentences.s132-s18: Seek nothing except devotion (service) before God; do not lay hands on (interfere with) good and evil except by your original/innate judgment.
sentences.s132-s19: He gave this answer: that from truth no lack should come into the inner affairs.
sentences.s132-s20: He kept speaking so, and there was no injustice; my life is not free from your name.
sentences.s132-s29: He became repentant; pain seized him and he endured suffering, and, having put it aside, he bestowed great treasures.
sentences.s132-s30: He kept rubbing his face on the dark earth, praying before the pure God.
sentences.s132-s31: After a little while had passed, weeping thus, the Creator forgave him.
sentences.s132-s32: Someone established a new justice in the world that became resplendent over the realms and the great.
sentences.s132-s25: Yellow water (tears) kept pouring from his eyes; he repeatedly remembered the Creator.
sentences.s132-s26: Out of shame he would not go out of the palace gate; his skin kept (as it were) saying, “Go — put it into your palm.”
sentences.s132-s27: Blood poured from his purified eyes; he continually sought forgiveness and guidance.
sentences.s132-s28: Because of shame he made the brave men's demeanour low; he strode proudly and shut the court's giving (i.e. closed off giving at the court).
sentences.s132-s5: When I heard of Kāvus in the world, I saw no one among the low-born or the great.
sentences.s132-s6: He has no wisdom: neither knowledge nor counsel; neither is his intellect in order, nor his heart in its place.
sentences.s132-s7: Then the original champions arrived, reproving, keen, and quarrelsome.
sentences.s132-s8: Goudarz said to him, 'A hospital is for you a place more beautiful than a town.'
sentences.s132-s9: You always give your place to the enemy; do not tell anyone your foolish counsel.
sentences.s132-s10: Such suffering and hardship befell you three times; from the ordeal you did not become a master.
sentences.s132-s11: You led the army to Mazandaran; look and see what hardships befell them.
sentences.s132-s12: Once again you became a guest of the enemy; you were an idol, and now you have become a Brahmin.
sentences.s132-s13: There remained in the world no one but the Pure God who would not be summoned by the proclamation of your sword.
sentences.s132-s14: You charged into battle across the earth, head to head; now you also came to (engage) Basman.
sentences.s132-s15: After you they will tell this tale, that a kingship arose to the lofty heaven.
sentences.s132-s16: so that he might look upon the moon and the sun, and count the stars one by one.
sentences.s132-s1: He kept begging pardon for his sin; armies searched for him on every side on my behalf.
sentences.s132-s2: Rostam, Giv, and Tūs heard the news of him; they set out with a hungry host and war-drums.
sentences.s132-s3: Goudarz the elder thus said to Rostam: 'Since my mother had nursed me to fullness (i.e. from the time my mother had fully nursed me)...'
sentences.s132-s4: I still see in the world the crowns and thrones of the Kian kings and the great men whose fortune is awake.
